marketState: bearish - weather premium peaking as fundamentals remain structurally oversupplied
summary: Natural gas is pricing in an acute heat-wave demand surge (next 7-day demand +44.6% vs 5-year norms, above the 5-year maximum), but the signal deteriorates sharply beyond week one (days 8-16 only +11.1% vs 5y) and 24-hour demand already fell 14.3 BCF/day, suggesting the seasonal peak may be in. Price at $3.279 sits $0.32 above the fair-value midpoint of $2.960 (band $2.46–$3.46), mildly elevated but within range across all forward horizons; notably the fair-value midpoint dips further to $2.945 in 7 days before recovering, widening the relative overvaluation near-term. Seasonal probabilities lean bearish — only 19% chance of being higher in four weeks with a –1.8% typical move — while storage surplus (+151 BCF vs 5-year average, plus a +6 BCF bearish injection surprise) adds structural headwind. The key bull offset is managed-money net positioning at just the 18th 5-year percentile, an historically extreme short stance that creates meaningful short-squeeze risk if the heat wave extends beyond current forecasts.

longTerm.rationale: Seasonality delivers only a 19% probability of being higher in four weeks with a –1.8% typical move (even with low reliability, the directional skew is clear). Storage at +151 BCF above the 5-year average with a bearish injection surprise constrains any fundamental rebalancing, and price remains above the fair-value midpoint ($2.945–$3.076) across all forward horizons while the weekly chart shows a bearish trend with a 50<200 EMA death cross. Entry at current levels or on a weather-driven pop to $3.35–$3.45 places the stop just above the fair-value band top ($3.46), giving approximately 1:2.5 R:R to TP1 at $3.00 and 1:4.5 to TP2 at $2.65 (using mid-entry $3.38 and stop $3.52).
longTerm.invalidation: A close above $3.52 (above the fair-value band top) confirmed by a weather-forecast extension into week 3+, or managed-money net flipping toward neutral (50th percentile+) indicating a squeeze is already underway.

shortTerm.rationale: The 1-7 day weather demand is still above the 5-year maximum (+44.6% vs 5y), keeping near-term price support intact and the Aug-Sep spread in slight backwardation ($0.053), while the 4-hour chart is bullish with positive MACD — a new short at current levels is premature. Simultaneously, demand dropped 14.3 BCF/day in 24 hours and weeks 2+ normalize sharply, so a new long into a fading heat dome also carries high mean-reversion risk; the 5m/15m timeframes are bearish with RSI at 34 and the daily has a 50<200 death cross, leaving direction genuinely unclear.
shortTerm.trigger: SHORT: enter $3.35–$3.45 if 24-hour demand continues dropping and 4h RSI rolls below 45, confirming heat premium is fading; or wait for a confirmed close below $3.15 for a weather-unwind breakdown trade with stop at $3.30. LONG (contra-squeeze only): enter on a 4h RSI bounce from 35–40 with a fresh weather-model upgrade extending heat beyond day 10.

keyDrivers: Near-term weather demand above 5-year maximum for 1-7 days (+44.6% vs 5y) supporting current front-month premium
Demand momentum rolling over — -14.3 BCF/day change in 24h and 8-16 day demand normalizing to just +11.1% vs 5y
Storage structurally bearish — +151 BCF above 5-year average with a surprise bearish injection (+6 BCF above forecast)
Seasonal headwinds — 19% probability of higher price in 4 weeks, -1.8% typical 4-week move, reliability flagged as Low
Managed-money net short at 18th 5-year percentile — extreme positioning creates material short-squeeze risk without a bullish catalyst
risks: Managed-money short squeeze: net positioning at 18th percentile is historically extreme; any weather-forecast extension or bullish supply shock could trigger rapid covering toward $3.46–$3.60+
Seasonal unreliability: 10-90th percentile spread of 51 pp this week means actual outcomes frequently deviate far from the -1.8% median, limiting directional confidence
Production data absent from snapshot — any unplanned supply curtailment, LNG export surge, or pipeline disruption could rapidly tighten the storage surplus and invalidate the bearish thesis
